items.title: Jacobian of a 2→2 coordinate map
items.explanation: Each row tracks how one output component changes with both inputs. A small displacement Δx = (0.01, 0) produces output change ≈ J·Δx ≈ (0.02, 0.02).

items.title: Hessian of a quadratic loss
items.scenario: Loss L(w₁, w₂) = (w₁ − 3)² + 2(w₂ + 1)². Gradient ∇L = (2(w₁−3), 4(w₂+1)). Hessian H = [[2, 0], [0, 4]]—constant and positive definite.
items.explanation: A quadratic with positive definite Hessian is a bowl-shaped surface. Newton's method would reach the minimum (3, −1) in one step because H is constant.

items.title: Saddle point from an indefinite Hessian
items.scenario: f(x, y) = x² − y². Critical point at (0, 0) with ∇f = 0. Hessian H = [[2, 0], [0, −2]] has eigenvalues +2 and −2.
items.explanation: Curvature is upward in x and downward in y—a classic saddle. Gradient descent can stall or crawl along flat directions; second-order information reveals the mixed curvature.

items.title: Layer Jacobian in a tiny neural net
items.scenario: One hidden layer: h = ReLU(W₁x + b₁), y = W₂h. The Jacobian of y with respect to x is J_y = W₂ · diag(ReLU′(z)) · W₁ (with subgradient at z = 0).
items.explanation: Backprop does not materialize this full product for every sample at once, but conceptually each layer contributes a Jacobian factor multiplied along the path from output to input.
